heading: MODULO SECONDO
card_summary: This chunk opens the second module of Zorzi's description of the heavenly city (the "città armoniosa"), comparing and reconciling the prophetic visions of Ezekiel and John of Patmos regarding its layout, dimensions, shape, gates, and squares. Zorzi explains why Ezekiel includes a temple while John excludes it (the Christian people have become the temple themselves), then performs detailed metrological calculations converting Ezekiel's reeds and John's stadia into Roman and Venetian measures. He argues that the numbers are not literal ("numero vocale") but symbolic and Pythagorean-Platonic ("numero naturale o formale"), drawing on Augustine, Pythagorean number theory, Platonic cosmology (Timaeus), and Kabbalistic interpretation of the aleph and the Tetragrammaton. The quadrilateral form of the city is explained through musical-harmonic ratios embedded in the quaternary, and the twelve gates (three per cardinal direction) encode the unity of Trinity and quaternity. The Hebrew phrase kol ha-neshamah tehallel Yah (Ps. 150:6) is transliterated and interpreted as the perpetual praise of purified souls before the divine essence.
essay:
# MODULO SECONDO — Disposizione della città armoniosa (pp. 2485–2497)

## 1. Ezechiele e Giovanni: perché l'uno include il tempio e l'altro lo esclude

Zorzi opens with a structural observation: Ezekiel divides the eschatological city into temple, city proper, and the whole Promised Land (in turn divided among twelve tribes), while John (Apoc. 21:22) explicitly excludes any temple: *"In essa non vidi alcun tempio."* The discrepancy is theologically motivated, not contradictory. Ezekiel describes the figurative people (the Israelite "stirpe figurale") who required a physical temple for prayer and sacrifice; John describes the Christian city whose inhabitants have already become the temple of God (1 Cor. 3:16-17). Zorzi cites John 4:23 (the Samaritan woman episode) and John 14:23 (*"Se uno osserverà la mia parola, il Padre mio lo amerà e verremo da lui e dimoreremo presso di lui"*) to show that the Christian believer is already the divine dwelling-place. In the eschatological condition, God and the Lamb are themselves the temple (Apoc. 21:22), and the blessed are received within that divine hospitality rather than merely hosting God as a guest.

## 2. Le dimensioni della città: dal numero vocale al numero formale

Zorzi undertakes careful metrological work. John measures the city at 12,000 stadia per side (perimeter: 48,000 stadia = 6,000 miles). Ezekiel measures it at 4,500 reeds per side (18,000 reeds total). Zorzi converts these using Jerome's equivalences: the Roman *passus* (15 palms) and the Venetian *passus* (20 palms, one quarter larger); a *calamus* contains 37 palms (6 cubits and 1 palm, with 1 cubit = 6 palms). This produces, in Venetian measure, a perimeter of roughly 1,530 miles for Ezekiel's city versus John's 6,000 miles — an apparent discrepancy. Zorzi explains this is because the New Jerusalem, "built by Christ as a bride adorned for her bridegroom," must be proportionally larger than the old Israel, as Christianity's family exceeds the figurative nation.

More important than literal measure, however, is the distinction between *numero vocale* and *numero naturale o formale*. Appealing to Augustine and to the Pythagoreans and Academics, Zorzi insists that Sacred Scripture uses numbers to express proportion, harmony, and mystery, not cardinality:

> *La sacra Scrittura, infatti (secondo la corretta opinione di Agostino), come del resto i pitagorici e gli accademici, se ne curano assai poco, poiché ciò a cui mirano è la proporzione risultante, che chiamano numero naturale o formale, e il divino nonché i misteri che ne promanano.*
<cite>p. 2321</cite>

The number 1,000 is the *cubus* — the solid extension of 10, the "numero completo." Beyond it no new number is possible, only repetition: it therefore signals ultimate perfection and completion. The 18,000 reeds of Ezekiel decompose as 1,000 × 18; the 18 refers to the two groups of nine choirs (nine angelic orders + nine corresponding human choirs) who are the city's inhabitants, following Gregory the Great. Alternatively, 18 = 12 + 6 = 12 × 6 = 72, the number of elders and judges of Israel, of Christ's disciples, and of the princes of heaven (the *secretiores theologi*), also the number of world languages assigned six per zodiacal sign. John's 12,000 and 144 (= 12²) deploy 1,000 as the number of perfect dilated fullness and 12 as the "primo numero eccedente." The total is expressed and completed in the Hebrew letter **aleph (א)**:

> *Il numero mille è espresso e trova il proprio compimento nella lettera א alef, che indica Dio stesso, e, allo stesso tempo, il primo principio di tutte le cose, nonché il loro termine ultimo, al quale alla fine sarà ricondotta ogni molteplicità, dopo aver terminato il ciclo stabilito per ogni cosa.*
<cite>p. 2323</cite>

## 3. La forma quadrata: armonia musicale e nome di Dio

Both Ezekiel and John describe the city as square. Zorzi grounds this in the Platonic doctrine of the *Timaeus*: the Demiurge established four-fold foundations for the world so that it would be solid, since solids require two mean proportionals rather than one. The four elements are bound through this mediation (fire–air–air–water–water–earth–earth–fire). The quaternary therefore encodes cosmic solidarity.

Within the number four, moreover, all musical consonances are found:

> *Nel quattro si ritrova la proporzione doppia, la tripla, la sesquialtera e la sesquiterza, dalle quali risulta il diapason, il bisdiapason, il diapente e il diatessaron e persino il tono, se si considerano quattro, tre e uno, che fanno otto, e ancora 4, 3 e 2, che fanno 9, cosicché il rapporto proporzionale tra 9 e 8 è di sesquiottava e compie il tono, in tal modo da esso risulta ogni armonia.*
<cite>p. 2323</cite>

This is Zorzi's characteristic fusion of Pythagorean harmonics with sacred architecture. The proportions 1:2 (diapason/octave), 2:3 (diapente/fifth), 3:4 (diatessaron/fourth), and 8:9 (tone/second) all emerge from the first four integers. The square city thus resonates with the entire musical system.

Over and above this arithmetic-musical dimension, the four-lettered divine Name (**Tetragrammaton**, יהוה) rules the four corners of the city — one letter per angle. Zorzi cites 2 Chronicles (*Parole dei giorni*): *"Ho scelto Gerusalemme perché il mio nome dimori in essa."* The blessed inhabitants are perpetually animated by this Name's influx to praise God, as the "corista regale" (the Psalms) declares. Zorzi then cites and transliterates Psalm 150:6 in Hebrew — *kol ha-neshamah tehallel Yah* — and offers a technical gloss:

> *kol ha-neshamah tehallel Yah, che tradotto alla lettera significa: 'Ogni ha-neshamah, ossia l'anima purificata e resa sublime o divina, lodi Yah, che è il nome che allude all'essenza divina, alla cui contemplazione sono ammessi i beati'.*
<cite>p. 2325</cite>

Zorzi notes the difficulty of rendering Hebrew *neshamah* (the highest, divinized soul) and *Yah* (the abbreviated divine Name) into any European vernacular — an admission of the limits of translation in Kabbalistic exegesis.

## 4. Le porte: Trinità nel quaternario, quaternario nella Trinità

The twelve gates (three per cardinal direction) encode the mutual implication of Trinity and quaternary. Three times four and four times three both yield twelve — the sacred number that governs tribes, apostles, zodiacal signs, angelic princes, and the elect family. The ternary is "woven into" the quaternary (and vice versa), so that baptism in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit is the necessary passage through any of the four directions of human life (youth, maturity, old age, decrepitude) or divine approach (goodness, piety, fear of judgment, merciful condescension). The twelve presiding angels correspond to priestly ministry (*Malachi*: the priest is "angel of the Lord of hosts") in the Church Militant and to angelic escort in the Church Triumphant — as in the parable of Lazarus (Luke 16:22). Christ's words to Peter about "more than twelve legions of angels" (Matt. 26:53) reinforce the duodecimal structure of the angelic host, whose total (Daniel) reaches "a thousand thousands and ten thousand myriads."

## 5. Le piazze: carità e sapienza

The final section treats the city's *plateae* (squares/streets), described by John as golden. Gold signifies charity (as previously established in Zorzi's system), so the public gathering places of heaven shine with mutual love — the sign by which Christ's disciples are recognized (John 13:35). At the center of the piazza stands the Tree of Life, flanked by the River of Living Water, encoding the complementary virtues of charity (gold) and wisdom (water), the latter drawn from the book of Wisdom: *"Et aqua sapientiae salutaris potavit illum."* Proverbs 1:20 (*Sapientia foris praedicat, in plateis dat...*) is invoked to show that wisdom announces itself precisely in the public squares. The chunk ends mid-sentence at this point.

## Thematic Significance

This chunk exemplifies Zorzi's "harmonia mundi" project at its most architecturally specific: prophetic visions become number-theoretical diagrams in which Pythagorean arithmetic, Platonic cosmology, Kabbalistic letter-mysticism (aleph, Tetragrammaton, neshamah/Yah), Gregorian angelology (nine choirs), and Augustinian hermeneutics all converge to decode the measurements of heaven. The recurring insistence on *numero formale* over *numero vocale* is the methodological spine of the entire third Cantico.
